content.text: Generative UI is the pattern where an AI model decides which interactive UI component to render, not just what text to say. Instead of returning a prose list of flight options, the model calls a render_flight_cards tool and the browser renders sortable, bookable cards. The model controls the layout; the developer controls the component library. This pattern bridges the gap between a chatbot and a dynamic application — users get text, interactive widgets, and structured data in a single conversation turn.

attrs.alt: Traditional chat returns plain text; generative UI renders interactive FlightCard components with sort, filter, and Book buttons inline

content.text: Core Idea: Tools as UI Contracts

The key insight is that tool call inputs are component props. Define tools whose input schemas exactly match your React component props. When the model calls render_flight_cards, the tool input is the props object; pass it directly to the FlightCards component without any execution or data fetching.
The server runs the model with the UI tools available and returns both text and a list of component specs. No tool execution happens on the server — the model's tool call IS the output.

from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic
import json

client = AsyncAnthropic()

GEN_UI_SYSTEM = """You are a helpful assistant with access to UI rendering tools.
When the user asks for data that can be displayed visually (flights, tables,
lists, forms, charts), prefer using the appropriate rendering tool over
returning plain text. The tool outputs are rendered as interactive components."""

async def gen_ui_agent(session_id: str, user_message: str, history: list) -> dict:
    """Run one agent turn; return text reply and/or tool-rendered UI components."""
    history.append({"role": "user", "content": user_message})

    response = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=4096,
        system=GEN_UI_SYSTEM,
        messages=history,
        tools=UI_TOOLS,
    )

    ui_components = []
    text_parts    = []

    for block in response.content:
        if block.type == "text":
            text_parts.append(block.text)
        elif block.type == "tool_use":
            # Tool input IS the component spec — no execution needed
            ui_components.append({
                "component": block.name,   # "render_flight_cards", etc.
                "props":     block.input,
                "tool_id":   block.id,
            })

    # Append assistant turn (include tool_use blocks for multi-turn context)
    history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})

    return {
        "text":   "\n".join(text_parts),
        "ui":     ui_components,
        "stop":   response.stop_reason,
    }

A single dispatcher component maps tool names to the correct React component and passes the tool input directly as props. Unrecognised tool names fall back to a JSON pretty-print so unknown components never cause a blank UI.
Each UI tool has a matching React component. The FlightCards component receives the model's structured output as props and adds client-side sort and filter without any additional server round-trips.
An assistant message can contain both text paragraphs and UI components. Render them in order: text first if it appears before the tool call, then the component, then any text that follows.
A render_form tool lets the model collect structured input from the user without the developer having to build a separate form page. The model specifies the fields; the form component renders them.

The model's tool input is untrusted data from a language model. Validate props against a Zod schema (TypeScript) or JSON Schema before passing them to components, especially for fields that affect navigation or data mutation.
Tool inputs stream as partial JSON during generation. Show a skeleton card while the model is still emitting the tool call, then swap in the real component when the block closes.

import pytest
from app import gen_ui_agent

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_model_calls_flight_tool(monkeypatch):
    from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic
    from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, MagicMock

    fake_content = [
        MagicMock(type="text", text="Here are your flights:"),
        MagicMock(type="tool_use", id="t1", name="render_flight_cards",
                  input={"flights": [{"id":"1","airline":"ANA","flight_no":"NH203",
                                      "departs":"2026-07-10T10:30:00Z",
                                      "arrives":"2026-07-11T14:25:00Z",
                                      "stops":0,"price_usd":680,"cabin":"economy"}]}),
    ]
    fake_response = MagicMock(content=fake_content, stop_reason="tool_use")
    monkeypatch.setattr("app.client.messages.create", AsyncMock(return_value=fake_response))

    result = await gen_ui_agent("sess1", "flights to Tokyo", [])
    assert result["text"] == "Here are your flights:"
    assert len(result["ui"]) == 1
    assert result["ui"][0]["component"] == "render_flight_cards"
    assert result["ui"][0]["props"]["flights"][0]["price_usd"] == 680

Generative UI turns tool call inputs into React component props, giving the model control over which interactive widget to display. The developer defines the component library and prop schemas; the model decides which component fits the user's request. This produces richer responses than plain text while keeping the component code deterministic and testable. Always validate props from the model before rendering to prevent unexpected values reaching the DOM or backend.
